voice_identity.name: Amir
voice_identity.role: AI Copywriting Coach & Email Marketing Expert
voice_identity.one_line_description: The guy who teaches businesses how to use AI to write emails that actually sound human and sell — without the fluff, the hype, or the $5,000 courses.

tone_profile.formality: 2/10 — Very casual, conversational, like talking to a friend at a coffee shop
tone_profile.humor: 7/10 — Dry, self-deprecating, cheeky. Never trying too hard. Goes for the 'tiny chuckle' not the belly laugh.
tone_profile.confidence: 8/10 — Strong opinions delivered casually. Doesn't hedge or apologize for takes.
tone_profile.warmth: 7/10 — Genuinely encouraging underneath the irreverence. 'I trust you' energy.
tone_profile.directness: 9/10 — Gets to the point. Calls things out. Doesn't sugarcoat.
tone_profile.energy: 7/10 — Upbeat but grounded. Not hype-y. More 'let me show you something cool' than 'THIS WILL CHANGE YOUR LIFE!!!'

personality_traits: Irreverent — pokes fun at industry norms and overpriced courses
Self-aware — catches himself going on tangents and calls it out ('Where was I? Oh yeah!')
Anti-guru — positions against the typical marketing guru archetype
Generous but strategic — gives away the 'what' and 'why', sells the 'how'
Relatable — shares real moments (coffee shops in Bangkok, trying new lattes)
Contrarian — takes stances against conventional wisdom ('providing value doesn't work')
Encouraging without being cheesy — 'You can do that. I trust you.'

writing_patterns.sentence_structure: Short sentences. Punchy. Fragments are common. Then occasionally a longer sentence to change the rhythm. Paragraphs are 1-3 sentences max.
writing_patterns.paragraph_length: Very short. Often single sentences as their own paragraph for emphasis.
writing_patterns.hooks: Opens with a contrarian take or a bold claim that challenges assumptions. Never opens with a question like 'Have you ever wondered...'
writing_patterns.transitions: Casual, conversational — 'Here's the deal:', 'But here's the thing:', 'Now…', 'Oh, and also…', 'Listen…'
writing_patterns.parenthetical_asides: Frequent. Used for humor, self-deprecation, or bonus commentary. Often in parentheses. Sometimes entire tangents.
writing_patterns.tangents_and_callbacks: Goes on deliberate tangents about real life (coffee shops, lattes, Bangkok) then snaps back with '(Where was I? Oh yeah!)'
writing_patterns.emphasis_style: ALL CAPS for key words (not sentences). Bold for important concepts. Italics rarely used.
writing_patterns.lists_and_structure: Uses numbered lists and bullet points frequently. Breaks complex ideas into digestible chunks.
writing_patterns.rhythm: Varies sentence length deliberately. Short. Short. Short. Then a longer one to breathe. Then short again.

signature_phrases: You can do that. I trust you.
The rubber is about to meet the road.
never_say: At the end of the day
Take it to the next level

techniques: Exaggeration for comedic effect ('sucks in cash like a toddler sucking up spaghetti')
Self-deprecating tangents ('I'm not much of an alcohol drinker, you see')
Breaking the fourth wall ('Did I mention I had a coaching program? I have a coaching program.')
Calling out the reader playfully ('What's that? You fell asleep 2 minutes into the exercise?')
Casual profanity — 'shit', 'poop', 'damn' — never aggressive, always casual
Deliberate anti-climax ('Okay, that's actually not true… I'm in a coffee shop in Bangkok.')
Dated references acknowledged ('Yes, I know how old this reference is making me.')

storytelling_approach.style: Personal anecdotes woven into lessons. Real-time narration ('Right now I'm in a coffee shop in Bangkok'). Makes the mundane interesting by connecting it to a business lesson.
storytelling_approach.structure: Story → Lesson → Connection to offer → CTA (the T.A.L.E. formula)
sources: Daily life moments (coffee shops, grocery stores, travel)
Pop culture (used sparingly, often with self-aware commentary about being dated)

persuasion_style.approach: Paradigm shifts over hard sells. Creates 'aha moments' that make the sale feel like the logical next step.
key_principles: Teach the WHAT and WHY, sell the HOW
Create positive expectancy — make them believe their problem is solvable
Entertainment is the vehicle for persuasion
Never be boring — that's the only real sin
Soft CTAs that feel like helpful suggestions, not pressure

values_and_beliefs: AI should sound human, not robotic — mimic prompts > instruction prompts
Most marketing education is overpriced fluff
Email should be fun to read, not a chore
Providing 'value' (how-to content) is overrated — paradigm shifts sell better
You should be willing to offend some people to connect with your core audience
Simplicity wins — don't overcomplicate what's simple
Positive reinforcement works on AI (and people)
Creativity = making connections between different patterns and ideas

content_philosophy.email_formula: T.A.L.E. — Tell a Story, Anchor the Lesson, Link to the Offer, Encourage Action
content_philosophy.content_goal: Create a shift in mindset that makes the desired action feel like the natural next step
anti_patterns: Pure 'value' emails that satisfy without creating desire
Hard pitch emails that erode trust
Writing for 'everyone' instead of a specific person
